title: What to know before using Anthemis cotula (Mayweed)?
intro: Anthemis cotula, also known as stinking chamomile, is a plant with a variety of medicinal properties. It has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and antibacterial properties. The plant contains several active compounds, including azulenes and flavonoids, which are believed to be responsible for its medicinal effects. In terms of horticultural conditions, Anthemis cotula is a hardy annual or short-lived perennial that thrives in full sun and well-drained soil. It can be found growing wild in fields, meadows, and along roadsides. The plant grows to be 1-2 feet tall and has finely divided, feathery leaves and small white or yellow daisy-like flowers. Despite its medicinal uses, Anthemis cotula is considered a weed in many areas due to its ability to spread rapidly and take over gardens and natural areas.
medicine_desc: Anthemis cotula has several medicinal uses, such as treating skin conditions, reducing inflammation, and alleviating pain. Its constituents include various volatile oils, such as camphor, borneol, and camphene, as well as flavonoids, coumarins, and tannins. Preparations of Anthemis cotula can be made into teas, tinctures, or ointments for topical use. However, it is important to note that Anthemis cotula can cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, and liver damage in some individuals. Therefore, it is crucial to consult with a healthcare professional before using Anthemis cotula and to perform a patch test before applying it to the skin. Additionally, pregnant women and individuals with liver or kidney disease should avoid using Anthemis cotula due to its potential harmful effects.
horticulture_desc: Anthemis cotula, also known as Mayweed or Dog Fennel, is a hardy annual plant that is native to Europe and parts of Asia. It typically grows to a height of 30-60 cm and has a spread of 45-60 cm. This plant prefers full sun and well-drained soil, and it can tolerate a wide range of soil types, from sandy to clay. Anthemis cotula is drought tolerant and can thrive in poor soil conditions, making it a low-maintenance option for gardeners. However, it does not tolerate wet or waterlogged soils. It is also intolerant of shade, so it should be planted in an area that receives at least 6 hours of sunlight per day.

When planting Anthemis cotula, it is best to sow the seeds directly into the garden in the spring, after the last frost date. The seeds should be sown thinly, about 2.5 cm apart, and covered with a thin layer of soil. The seeds will germinate in 14-21 days. If starting the seeds indoors, they should be sown in late winter or early spring, about 6-8 weeks before the last frost date. The seedlings should be transplanted into the garden after the last frost date, spacing them about 30-45 cm apart.

Anthemis cotula is a low-maintenance plant that does not require a lot of care. However, it does benefit from regular watering, especially during dry spells. The plant should be watered deeply, allowing the water to reach the roots. It is important to avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot. The plant also benefits from regular deadheading, which encourages new blooms and keeps the plant looking tidy. Fertilizer is not necessary, as Anthemis cotula is able to thrive in poor soil conditions.

Anthemis cotula is typically grown for its attractive, yellow, daisy-like flowers. The flowers are produced in abundance throughout the summer and are highly attractive to bees and butterflies. The flowers can be harvested for use in flower arrangements or for drying. To dry the flowers, cut the stems when the flowers are in full bloom and hang them upside down in a dark, dry, well-ventilated area. The flowers should be completely dry in about two weeks.

Anthemis cotula is relatively pest and disease-free, but it can be susceptible to powdery mildew, especially in humid conditions. To prevent powdery mildew, it is important to ensure that the plant has good air circulation and to avoid overwatering. If powdery mildew does occur, it can be treated with a fungicide. The plant can also be affected by aphids, which can be controlled by introducing beneficial insects such as ladybugs or by using insecticidal soap. Slugs and snails can also be a problem, but they can be controlled by using slug pellets or by creating a barrier around the plant with sharp grit or eggshells.
botany_desc: Anthemis cotula, commonly known as stinking chamomile or mayweed, is a species of flowering plant in the daisy family, Asteraceae. It is native to Europe and North Africa but has been introduced and naturalized in many parts of the world, including North America. Anthemis cotula is an annual plant that grows to a height of 30-60 cm and has a taproot system. It is a member of the tribe Anthemideae, which includes other chamomile species. The genus Anthemis contains about 200 species, but Anthemis cotula is one of the most widely distributed and invasive.

Anthemis cotula has a characteristic morphology that distinguishes it from other Anthemis species. It has a branched stem that is covered with fine hairs, and its leaves are alternate, bi-pinnate, and also covered with hairs. The leaves have a strong, unpleasant odor when crushed, which gives the plant its common name of stinking chamomile. The flowers are small, white, and clustered in terminal heads. Each flower head has a center disk of tiny tubular florets surrounded by white ray florets. The flowers bloom from June to October and are attractive to bees and butterflies.

Anthemis cotula has several variant names, including dog fennel, stinkweed, and mayweed. These names reflect the plant's strong odor and its ability to invade crops and pastures. Anthemis cotula is often mistaken for other Anthemis species, such as Anthemis nobilis, the Roman chamomile, or Anthemis tinctoria, the golden chamomile. However, Anthemis cotula can be distinguished by its stronger odor, finer hairs on the stem and leaves, and smaller flower heads. Anthemis cotula is also more invasive and aggressive than other Anthemis species, making it a problematic weed in many areas.

Anthemis cotula is native to Europe and North Africa but has been introduced and naturalized in many parts of the world, including North America, Australia, and New Zealand. It prefers disturbed habitats, such as roadsides, fields, and waste areas, and can tolerate a wide range of soil types and conditions. Anthemis cotula is often found in areas with dry summers and mild winters, such as the Mediterranean region. It can also be found in coastal areas, where it can tolerate salt spray and sandy soils. Anthemis cotula is a successful invasive species due to its ability to produce large numbers of seeds and its tolerance of a wide range of environmental conditions.

Anthemis cotula has a life cycle that is typical of annual plants. It reproduces by seed, and each plant can produce up to 20,000 seeds. The seeds are small, brown, and have a feathery pappus that aids in wind dispersal. The seeds can remain viable in the soil for up to five years, making Anthemis cotula a persistent and difficult-to-control weed. Anthemis cotula germinates in the spring and produces flowers and seeds in the summer. The seeds are mature in the fall and are dispersed by the wind, often in the late summer and early fall. Anthemis cotula can also reproduce vegetatively, by fragmentation of the root system, which can lead to the formation of new plants. This ability to reproduce both sexually and asexually makes Anthemis cotula a successful and adaptable species.
intro_desc: Anthemis cotula, also known as stinking chamomile, is a plant with a variety of medicinal properties. It has been used in traditional medicine to treat skin conditions, inflammation, and respiratory issues. The plant contains several active compounds, including azulenes and flavonoids, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antispasmodic effects. Anthemis cotula prefers well-drained soil and full sun, and it can tolerate dry conditions. It is a small, annual herb that grows to about 30 cm in height, with finely divided, feathery leaves and small, yellow, daisy-like flowers. Despite its medicinal uses, it is important to note that Anthemis cotula can also cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some people, and it should be used with caution.
benefits_desc: Anthemis cotula has many health benefits, such as its anti-inflammatory properties which can help alleviate symptoms of conditions like arthritis and skin irritations. Its antimicrobial properties also make it useful for treating wounds and preventing infections. Moreover, mayweed has been used in traditional medicine to treat digestive issues, as it can help stimulate appetite and alleviate symptoms of indigestion. Additionally, Anthemis cotula has been found to have antioxidant properties, which can help protect the body against damage from free radicals and support overall health. Lastly, this herb has been used to treat anxiety and promote relaxation, making it a potential natural remedy for stress relief.
constituents_desc: Anthemis cotula has many active constituents, such as anthemidin, which is known for its anti-inflammatory and antipyretic properties. This plant also contains costic acid, which has been found to have antimicrobial and antifungal effects. Additionally, the presence of camphor and borneol in mayweed contributes to its use as a natural remedy for ailments such as coughs, colds, and digestive issues. The plant's flavonoid content, particularly luteolin and quercetin, have been studied for their potential benefits in treating allergies, inflammation, and even cancer. Furthermore, the essential oil of Anthemis cotula contains various terpenes, such as alpha-pinene and beta-pinene, which have been found to have various medicinal properties, including anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antispasmodic effects.
preparations_desc: Anthemis cotula has many medicinal preparations, such as essential oils, extracts, and dried flower heads. The flowers and leaves of the plant are used to make these preparations. One common use of mayweed is as a topical treatment for skin conditions, including wounds, rashes, and eczema. The essential oil, obtained from the flowers, is also used in aromatherapy for its calming and soothing properties. Additionally, mayweed extracts have been used historically in traditional medicine as a remedy for digestive issues, such as indigestion and stomach cramps. Dried flower heads can be used to make teas, which are believed to have diuretic and anti-inflammatory effects. Overall, mayweed has a long history of use in medicinal preparations, with various parts of the plant utilized for different applications.
side_effects_desc: Anthemis cotula can have some side effects if used improperly, such as skin irritation and inflammation. It may cause allergic reactions in some individuals, resulting in contact dermatitis with symptoms like redness, itching, and blistering. If ingested, it can lead to gastrointestinal disturbances, such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. In extreme cases, it may even affect the nervous system, causing symptoms like tremors, seizures, and loss of consciousness. Additionally, prolonged use or high doses of mayweed can result in photosensitivity, making the skin more sensitive to sunlight and leading to sunburn or other skin damage.
precautions_desc: You should take some precautions when using Anthemis cotula for medicinal purposes, such as properly identifying the plant to ensure you have the correct species, as there are toxic look-alikes. It's essential to use it under the guidance of a healthcare professional, as inappropriate use can lead to ineffective treatment or even worsening of symptoms. When preparing mayweed for medicinal use, only use the aerial parts of the plant and avoid any parts that appear diseased or discolored. Additionally, it's crucial to follow proper dosage instructions carefully, as exceeding recommended dosages can cause irritation or other adverse reactions. Lastly, always store Anthemis cotula in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight to preserve its medicinal properties.
history_desc: Anthemis cotula, also known as stinking chamomile or dog fennel, has a long history of medicinal use dating back thousands of years. The ancient Greeks and Romans used it to treat a variety of ailments, including fever, cough, and skin conditions. The plant contains several active compounds, including azulene, that have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. In the Middle Ages, it was used to induce abortion and was known as the "beggar's blanket" or "gypsy's rag" because of its association with poverty and desperation. In the 19th century, it was used in Western herbal medicine as a treatment for digestive disorders, menstrual cramps, and skin conditions. However, its use as a medicinal herb has declined in modern times due to its toxicity and the availability of safer and more effective treatments.

The mythology of Anthemis cotula is intertwined with its medicinal uses. In ancient Greece, it was associated with the goddess Artemis, the goddess of the hunt, the wilderness, and childbirth. Artemis was known for her healing powers, and Anthemis cotula was one of the herbs used in her rituals. The plant's ability to induce abortion and relieve menstrual cramps made it a symbol of female reproductive power and autonomy. In Roman mythology, it was associated with the goddess Diana, the Roman equivalent of Artemis. The plant's pungent smell was said to attract snakes, and it was used in rituals to protect against snakebites and other venomous creatures.

Anthemis cotula was used in a variety of ancient rituals and ceremonies. In ancient Greece, it was used in purification rituals and as an offering to the gods. The plant's association with Artemis made it a powerful symbol of female power and autonomy, and it was used in rituals to promote fertility and protect women during childbirth. In Roman times, it was used in rituals to protect against evil spirits and the evil eye. The plant's pungent smell was believed to ward off negative energy and attract positive vibes. It was also used in funeral rites to help the deceased transition to the afterlife.

Anthemis cotula has been mentioned in literature throughout history. In ancient Greece, it was mentioned in the works of Hippocrates, the father of Western medicine, and Dioscorides, a Greek physician and botanist. In the Middle Ages, it was mentioned in the works of European herbalists, including John Gerard and Nicholas Culpeper. In modern times, it has been mentioned in works of fiction, including "The Secret Garden" by Frances Hodgson Burnett and "The Physick Book of Deliverance Dane" by Katherine Howe. Its use as a medicinal herb and its association with witchcraft and the supernatural have made it a popular subject in literature and popular culture.

Anthemis cotula has a rich symbolism that reflects its medicinal uses, mythological associations, and historical rituals. Its pungent smell and association with snakes make it a symbol of transformation and healing. Its association with Artemis and Diana make it a symbol of female power and autonomy. Its use in purification rituals and as an offering to the gods make it a symbol of spiritual cleansing and connection to the divine. Its association with witchcraft and the supernatural make it a symbol of the unknown and the mysterious. Overall, Anthemis cotula is a complex and multifaceted symbol that reflects the many facets of human experience and culture.
history_intro_desc: Anthemis cotula has several historical medicinal uses, such as being used to treat a variety of ailments including toothache, rheumatism, and earache. In Greek mythology, it was dedicated to the goddess Artemis and was used in rituals to promote fertility. Ancient rituals also included burning the plant to ward off evil spirits. Literature from the Middle Ages references mayweed as a cure for poisonous bites and stings. The symbolism of Anthemis cotula is often associated with protection and purification due to its use in ancient rituals.
